title: Next Men's World Cup set for three continents. What to know about FIFA 2030
description: Come the next World Cup, Spain will defend its current champion title on home soil.
fullText:
In the next edition of the FIFA Men's World Cup, Spain will defend its championship on home soil, as the European country will host World Cup 2030 jointly with Portugal and Morocco.

The next edition of the Men's World Cup will take place in the Mediterranean, particularly in Morocco, Portugal and Spain, the FIFA Council confirmed.

The 2030 tournament will mark the 100 anniversary since the first World Cup was contested in Uruguay in 1930. To commemorate a century of history, FIFA agreed to host a centenary celebration in the Southern Cone of South America, specifically in Paraguay, Uruguay and Argentina. All three countries are a few hours drive away from each other.

One game will be played in each of the South American countries. All other matches will take place in the Mediterranean host countries.

"In 2030 the FIFA World Cup will unite three continents and six countries, inviting the entire world to join in the celebration of the beautiful game," FIFA stated.

The centenary ceremony will take place in Uruguay's capital, Montevideo, five hours away from Argentina's capital, Buenos Aires.

FIFA said that the national soccer team of each of the six host countries will automatically qualify for the tournament.

Saudi Arabia will host the 2034 Men's World Cup across five host cities. The country will be the only hosting nation.

Juan Carlos Castillo is a New Jersey-based trending reporter for the USA Today network. He covers weather, and national events focusing on how they affect New Jersey, Pennsylvania and Delaware.

This article originally appeared on Asbury Park Press: Next Men's World Cup set for three continents. What to know about FIFA 2030

title: ‘I’m A Top Contender’ — Luke Lessei Reasserts ONE World Title Credentials After Rebound Win Over Mohamed Younes Rabah
description: "The Chef" stirred the pot about what comes next after his big win this past Friday.
fullText:
Luke “The Chef” Lessei has always been at his most dangerous when the doubters are loudest, and ONE Fight Night 45 was the most emphatic evidence yet.

Hungry to respond after a recent setback, the Iowa native delivered a commanding unanimous decision over “The Eagle” Mohamed Younes Rabah in the featherweight Muay Thai main event, which aired live in U.S. primetime from Bangkok’s iconic Lumpinee Stadium on Friday, July 17.

Lessei’s striking has always been a genuinely captivating blend of surgical precision and explosive flair, but consistency has sometimes been the missing ingredient.

The painful TKO defeat to now-ONE Interim Featherweight Muay Thai World Champion Nico “King of the North” Carrillo last November raised uncomfortable questions about his place among the division’s elite.

Against Rabah, a sharper, more deliberate Lessei stepped into the ring, looking every bit like a fighter reborn. Behind that renewed composure lay the defining trait of a warrior who never buckles when his back is against the wall.

“It felt great. I’m always happy to get a win. Fun fact, I’ve actually never lost two fights in a row. So I got that streak alive coming off a loss against a champion and a win against a top contender like Younes, who’s one of my friends.”

The opening round did not unfold as Lessei had envisioned, with Rabah finding his range quickly and seizing the advantage with clean, punishing combinations.

Sensing the momentum shifting, the 30-year-old tactician absorbed a crucial corner adjustment that changed the course of the fight entirely.

“My coach started telling me to trust my boxing and double up my hands a little bit more. I started to find my range with the jab, and it just brought me more confidence.

“I realized that the long straight shots were going to win me the rest of that fight, so I was just trusting in my actual reach, trusting in my coach.”

Lessei took full control as the fight progressed, his shots landing with greater frequency and force. A thunderous hook in the final stanza rocked Rabah badly, briefly buckling the Algerian’s knees before his toughness kept him upright.

Although all three judges awarded him the nod, Lessei’s exacting nature craves something far more emphatic. The cerebral finisher wanted something more decisive, and he is already thinking about how to close that gap.

“There was one shot in the third round — a big hook, and my coach tells me like it should have been an eight-count. I wish I had gotten that eight-count. I could have smelled blood and went for the kill, chasing the 50K bonus.

“But it is what it is. I’m happy with the shots that I landed. I can always improve. I can always do better, and I think I could push the pace even more.”

Luke Lessei’s win over Mohamed Younes Rabah means far more than a return to form, thrusting him firmly back into featherweight contention.

Looking ahead, three names stand out. A rematch with Nico Carrillo tops the list, while Bampara Kouyate, who handed him a buzzer-beater knockout, remains unforgotten. The American striker also remains deeply intrigued by a potential showdown with Thai standout Shadow Mavinn.

Lessei’s desire to test his mettle against the division’s best burns so deeply that he would gladly do so under kickboxing rules if need be:

“I just heard [Shadow] has a broken jaw right now, so I’m not sure how soon he’ll be back, but all three of the names — Bambi, Nico, Shadow, whoever.

“I’ve always been interested in putting the big gloves back on, doing kickboxing, since there’s no big-glove Muay Thai. But, yeah, I love banging in whatever style it is for striking.”

With reigning ONE Featherweight Muay Thai World Champion Tawanchai PK Saenchai sidelined through injury, the division is ripe with opportunity — and Lessei has every intention of seizing it.

For now, “The Chef” is savoring Friday night’s result. The 26 pounds of gold remain the ultimate destination, and every performance is another step toward it.

“A chance to fight for the belt is always at the top of the list, so I can’t really decide. I’m just down to fight whoever. I just love fighting for ONE. I love performing for my fans.

“I want to make sure I enjoy my win, and I focus on being present, but I think I’m a top contender. I want to keep reminding people with good performances.”

title: Barcelona expecting a public gesture from priority target with World Cup over
description: The FIFA World Cup final was all that was hindering progress in the Barcelona-Julian Alvarez saga, for the player was an integral member of Lionel Scaloni’s side and did not wish to cause any distur...
fullText:
The FIFA World Cup final was all that was hindering progress in the Barcelona-Julian Alvarez saga, for the player was an integral member of Lionel Scaloni’s side and did not wish to cause any disturbance in the dressing room during the tournament.

In recent days, bridges between Alvarez and Atletico Madrid took a big hit, especially after the Spanish club’s CEO openly declared that the player was not for sale at any price.

He openly called out Barcelona and Joan Laporta for their approaches and maintained that the player would not leave, thereby breaking a promise they made to the player last season.

As reported by SPORT now, the moment of truth has now arrived in the Alvarez saga now that the World Cup is complete.

Alvarez must now take a stand. (Photo by Elsa/Getty Images)

Alvarez is furious with Atletico Madrid for going back on their word, for they had promised to enable a departure should the player desire it at the end of the season.

Now that the international tournament is complete, there is an expectation within Barcelona that the Argentine is willing to step forward and make a move from his side.

The ball is now in Alvarez’s court, and while a departure is still complicated, a public announcement of his desire to leave and play for Barcelona can change a lot.

It will put pressure on Diego Simeone’s side and force the negotiating doors open.

If the Spanish outlet is to be believed, Alvarez wishes to clarify a lot of hidden truths about his situation and how he has been wronged. The time has now arrived, and Barcelona are expecting the said gesture from his side.

Barcelona will not wait for Alvarez forever. (Photo by Eric Alonso/Getty Images)

The World Cup eclipsed a major part of the ongoing summer transfer window, and while it is now past us, so is the majority of the negotiating window clubs had.

Barcelona are thus looking to be smart about its situation and do not wish to throw all their eggs in one basket.

The Argentine striker is the dream signing, but the board have reportedly set a deadline of 31st July to decide on his transfer.

Should no gesture arrive and Atletico Madrid continue to keep a firm stance, they will start looking for alternative options.

title: 'The Era of Red, Blue & Gold!': IPL champions RCB shares unique congratulatory message for FIFA World Cup winners Spain
description: With Spain lifting the FIFA World Cup yesterday, current and back-to-back IPL champions, RCB shared a unique congratulating message for the world champions.
fullText:
'The Era of Red, Blue & Gold!': IPL champions RCB shares unique congratulatory message for FIFA World Cup winners Spain originally appeared on Cricket News. Add Cricket News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

RCB congratulated Spain on their FIFA World Cup triumph with a themed post.

Ferran Torres scored in extra time as Spain beat Argentina 1-0 in New Jersey.

Both RCB and Spain have now completed back-to-back major title successes.

Spain were crowned world champions on Sunday night, with substitute Ferran Torres striking in the 106th minute to seal a 1-0 win over Argentina at the New York New Jersey Stadium in East Rutherford.

It was a final Spain dominated throughout, managing 20 shots to Argentina's two and taking the game away from a side reduced to 10 men after Enzo Fernandez was sent off for a second bookable offence in stoppage time.

The tributes arrived from every corner of the world of sport, and among the more unusual came from Indian cricket. Royal Challengers Bengaluru, the back-to-back IPL champions, posted a message linking their own success with Spain's.

MORE: Andy Flower committed to RCB: IPL-winning coach explains why he has ruled himself out of England coaching role

The franchise shared an image combining Spain's celebrations with their own title-winning moments from 2025 and 2026, drawing a line between two teams who have each retained their status at the top.

"Congratulations to Spain on winning the FIFA WC. The Era of Red, Blue & Gold!" the RCB account posted on X.

Congratulations to Spain on winning the FIFA WC. The Era of Red, Blue & Gold! ⭐️🏆#PlayBold#ನಮ್ಮRCBpic.twitter.com/1wKxZw0dcR

— Royal Challengers Bengaluru (@RCBTweets) July 20, 2026

The colour reference nods to the red and gold that both Spain and RCB carry, while the blue points to the Barcelona core running through this Spain side, with Torres, Lamine Yamal and Pau Cubarsi all products of the Catalan club.

The parallel between the two sides holds up. RCB ended an 18-year wait for a maiden IPL title in 2025 before defending it in 2026, while Spain followed their Euro 2024 triumph with a second World Cup crown, 16 years after their first in 2010.

MORE: Full list of injuries suffered by India stars in 2026 as concerns of fitness raised

Franchise accounts posting about other sports usually feel forced, but this one has a genuine thread running through it. Both teams spent years being described as talented underachievers before finally converting that potential into silverware.

The comparison also lands on the method rather than just the results. Spain won this tournament by control, conceding a single goal across the entire competition, while RCB's back-to-back titles were built on Andy Flower turning a batting-heavy side into a balanced one.

There is a commercial logic, too. RCB has the largest social following of any IPL franchise, and a World Cup final watched by hundreds of millions offers an obvious moment to reach beyond cricket, particularly with Indian audiences increasingly following European football.

title: Ballon d’Or 2026 odds: Harry Kane leads the betting after World Cup final
description: The Ballon d’Or race has taken another dramatic turn following the conclusion of the 2026 World Cup, with Harry Kane now heading the betting after another remarkable campaign for club and country.Sp...
fullText:
The Ballon d’Or race has taken another dramatic turn following the conclusion of the 2026 World Cup, with Harry Kane now heading the betting after another remarkable campaign for club and country.

Spain’s World Cup triumph has also seen Lamine Yamal and Rodri shorten significantly in the market after starring throughout the tournament, while Lionel Messi’s run to a second consecutive World Cup final keeps the Argentine firmly among the favourites.

Below, we assess the leading contenders in the latest Ballon d’Or betting.

Harry Kane has moved to the head of the market after arguably the finest season of his career.

The England captain finally ended his wait for major silverware by helping Bayern Munich to another Bundesliga title before leading the Three Lions to the World Cup semi-finals. Kane once again finished among Europe’s leading goalscorers, combining relentless finishing with his trademark creativity.

Although England fell short against Argentina, Kane’s consistency across the entire campaign leaves him as the bookmaker’s favourite for football’s biggest individual prize.

Lamine Yamal continues to rewrite the record books.

Still only a teenager, the Barcelona winger inspired Spain to World Cup glory with another series of match-winning performances on the biggest stage. His creativity, dribbling and composure regularly separated him from defenders throughout the tournament, cementing his status as one of the world’s elite attackers.

Winning the World Cup has propelled Yamal into serious Ballon d’Or contention, and few players possess stronger momentum heading into the final months of voting.

Lionel Messi was priced at 50/1 to win the Ballon d’Or when the 2026 World Cup got underway, but his stunning performances for Argentina have dramatically changed the picture.

The 39-year-old has scored eight goals in six matches so far, inspiring the defending champions to another World Cup Final, and shrunk his Ballon d’Or odds to 4/1.

The Argentina captain guided the reigning champions to a second successive World Cup final and once again produced decisive moments throughout the tournament. His creativity remained central to Lionel Scaloni’s side, while his influence on and off the pitch reinforced his standing as one of the game’s greatest players.

Although Argentina narrowly missed out on retaining their title, Messi’s performances ensure he remains firmly in the Ballon d’Or conversation.

Rodri reminded everyone why he remains one of football’s most influential players.

The Manchester City midfielder controlled games throughout Spain’s victorious World Cup campaign and capped another outstanding year by lifting the biggest trophy in international football. His ability to dictate possession, break up attacks and deliver in decisive moments makes him one of the leading candidates.

Few midfielders combine consistency and silverware as effectively as Rodri, making 4/1 a fair reflection of his credentials.

Kylian Mbappe rarely stays out of Ballon d’Or discussions for long.

The France captain enjoyed another prolific season at club level and continued to deliver on the international stage despite Les Bleus falling short of another World Cup final. His pace, finishing and ability to decide the biggest matches keep him among the leading contenders. Mbappe became the all-time World Cup top goalscorer this summer and now has 22 goals to his name.

Another strong domestic campaign means Mbappe cannot be discounted despite drifting slightly in the betting.

Jude Bellingham (20/1) enjoyed another excellent campaign despite England’s semi-final exit, while Ousmane Dembele (20/1) remains an outsider after another impressive season.

Fabian Ruiz (25/1) enhanced his reputation during Spain’s successful World Cup campaign, with Michael Olise (25/1) also attracting support following an outstanding breakthrough year for club and country.

Erling Haaland (33/1) will hope another fast start to the season keeps him in contention, while Khvicha Kvaratskhelia (33/1) and Pau Cubarsi (33/1) complete the current list of leading outsiders

title: Spain Humiliates Trump After He Refuses to Get Out of Victory Photos
description: Donald Trump presented Spain’s World Cup team with the trophy ... and then refused to leave.
fullText:
Spain’s World Cup-winning team has made it abundantly clear that they did not want Donald Trump in their victory photos.

The official account for Spain’s national football league shared an altered image of the country’s celebration in New Jersey Sunday, snipping the U.S. president out of photos after he refused to leave the dais. The FIFA World Cup account also cropped Trump out of the shot, leaving only the president’s shoulder in view.

Trump handed the World Cup trophy to Spain’s team alongside FIFA President Gianni Infantino, after they beat Argentina 1-0. But the U.S. president then refused to leave the picture, apparently trying to run out time before the traditional trophy lift by chatting with the players as he jockeyed for a prime position in front of the group.

Spain’s team, however, was not having it. Video footage of the historic celebration captured several players politely pushing Trump away as they prepared for their official photograph.

Spain’s players refused to celebrate with Trump after the U.S. president attempted to insert himself into the middle of their victory photo. He was politely pushed aside. pic.twitter.com/x82yxxCZgw

— Anonymous (@YourAnonCentral) July 20, 2026

Yet Trump stayed anyway, lingering beside the team even after repeated attempts by Infantino to pull him off the stage. He finally exited only after the team lifted its trophy and blew the confetti cannons in a glaring attempt to ensure he’d be included in the photograph.

His insistence on remaining in frame followed weeks of scathing remarks by Trump against Spain and its prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, over NATO defense spending. Trump has referred to Spain as “hopeless,” derided its people as “bad,” and also threatened to “cut off all trade” with the longtime U.S. ally as he ramped up his NATO disputes. In the months prior to that, he also raged against Spain after they refused to let the U.S. military use Spanish bases to bomb Iran.

None of that hostility was on display after the game, however—at least publicly. Back at Joint Base Andrews, Trump said he had spoken to Spain and “congratulated them on having a great team.” He also noted that he had “no tension” with the country’s leadership.

“I have no tension with anybody,” Trump remarked cavalierly.

title: Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin boycotts World Cup final in protest against Fifa
description: Fifa has come under heavy scrutiny during the 2026 World Cup, with the debacle surrounding Folarin Balogun just one aspect of Uefa’s discontent
fullText:
Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin stayed away from the World Cup final in protest, with the Folarin Balogun affair understood to be just one aspect of his organisation’s discontent with Fifa.

The Times was first to report on Monday that the Slovenian had stayed away, with the relationship between European football’s governing body and the game’s global federation under severe strain.

It’s understood Ceferin chose not to attend the match in New Jersey, with the Uefa president’s unhappiness understood to cover a number of aspects of Fifa’s handling of the World Cup.

Uefa was publicly critical of the decision by Fifa’s disciplinary committee to suspend a one-match ban for United States striker Balogun, calling it “unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable” and saying it had “crossed a red line”.

The suspension of the ban followed a call from US president Donald Trump to Fifa president Gianni Infantino asking for a “review” of Balogun’s red card, raising questions of political influence in Fifa’s decision-making.

That influence certainly did not appear to be reciprocal, with Fifa powerless to prevent the US authorities sending home Somali referee Omar Artan before the tournament had even started. Uefa subsequently appointed Artan to officiate next month’s Uefa Super Cup final.

Fifa also failed to ensure equitable treatment for Iran compared to the other 47 nations, with the Asian nation subject to strict conditions on their entry to and exit from the US. Their coach, Amir Ghalenoei, said his team were the “most oppressed” at the World Cup, while US Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin said he “danced a happy dance” when the team were eliminated.

There is also discontent at Uefa over Fifa’s apparent willingness to bend or break football’s laws at the tournament – with hydration breaks effectively splitting matches into four quarters and allowing broadcasters to cut to adverts, and the 27-minute half-time interval at the World Cup final well in excess of the maximum 15 minutes permitted.

There are also no plans for Uefa to adopt other changes introduced for the World Cup – such as the interpretation of ‘mistaken identity’ VAR protocol which led to Switzerland’s Breel Embolo being sent off in the quarter-final against Argentina, or the dismissal of players who cover their mouths when talking to an opponent.

Ceferin has also been publicly critical of Fifa’s ticket pricing strategy for the tournament, with Uefa having revealed that 40 per cent of tickets for Euro 2028 will be in the most affordable categories and priced at around £30 or £60, and having also pledged not to adopt dynamic pricing.

Ceferin did attend the opening match of the tournament in Mexico City on 11 June.

title: Lamine Yamal celebrates World Cup win with little brother who charmed fans
description: The three-year-old brother of Yamal has been hailed one of Spain's World Cup breakout stars by fans
fullText:
Spanish footballer Lamine Yamal may have lifted the World Cup but it is his little brother Kenye who appears to have stolen the spotlight.

The three-year-old quickly become an internet sensation after footage of his exuberant celebrations following Spain's 1-0 victory over Argentina spread across social media on Sunday.

While the footballer, just 19 himself, continued his rise by helping Spain achieve football's biggest prize, attention swiftly turned to his younger brother's joyful reactions from the sidelines.

From sticking out his tongue on the big screen to wildly cheering on his older brother, Kenye has been hailed by fans as one of the unexpected breakout stars of the 2026 tournament.

Spain triumphed in a battle of endurance, beating Argentina with a goal in the 106th minute at the New York New Jersey Stadium.

The team's second World Cup win has led to Lamine Yamal becoming the youngest player ever to win both the European Championship and the World Cup.

His younger brother Kenye has become a regular fixture both on and off the pitch - often featuring in Lamine Yamal's TikToks and charming fans from around the world.

The three-year-old, who appears keen to follow in his big brother's footsteps, is the son of Lamine Yamal's mother Sheila Ebana.

Speaking about Kenye, Lamine Yamal previously told outlets: "I'm moved when I see my little brother this happy, as well as seeing my mum and friends living the life they always dreamed of.

"My little brother means everything to me. I am in love with him; it feels like he is my son."

In what could be seen as a symbolic passing of the torch, Lamine Yamal went head to head with Argentinan football legend Lionel Messi for the first time during the World Cup final.

The two had crossed paths years earlier in 2007 when Messi, then 20, was seen cradling and bathing a tiny baby boy - Lamine Yamal - after his mother won a competition to meet a Barcelona first-team player.

Days ahead of Sunday's final, Messi, now 39, described Lamine Yamal as "one of the best" footballers right now.

"He's a tremendous player - a global star. He's 19 years old and has his whole career ahead of him. I wish him the best, but we'll give it our all to ensure he doesn't become champion this time," he added.

As the full-time whistle blew, cementing Spain's victory, the pair could be seen embracing on the pitch.

fullText:
Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material

Cases of predators coercing children into producing sexual photos and videos of themselves have surged globally in the past year, a cruel phenomenon known as “sadistic online exploitation”, child safety experts and law enforcement officials warn.

In cases of sadistic online exploitation, perpetrators target vulnerable teens on social media platforms and online games. These predators then compel their victims into producing child sexual abuse material (CSAM), harming themselves and others, or, in extreme cases, attempting suicide.

“We often see that the offenders will communicate with that child and, through that initial communication, ask them for nude or explicit imagery of themselves,” said Kathryn Rifenbark, a director at the National Center of Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC). “Then they’re using that as a leverage to cause the child to harm themselves or harm other children or other people in their home or create more explicit imagery of them or other children in their home.”

Recent cases include the June sentencing of a 21-year-old Maryland man to 30 years in prison for sexually exploiting 10 female minors and cyberstalking.

The perpetrator “used the internet to create and share extreme content – such as gore, violence, and child sexual abuse material – to extort and blackmail vulnerable teenagers”, and the man sexually exploited his victims and encouraged them to engage in self-harm, according to a Department of Justice press release.

The NCMEC received more than 3,000 reports of sadistic online exploitation worldwide in 2025, a 125% increase from the previous year. But those cases represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say, because the NCMEC is only notified if child sexual abuse material has been detected and reported.

Predators targeting children often belong to online networks, and are often teens or young adults themselves. To recruit their victims, the perpetrators engage with video games popular with children, such as Roblox and Minecraft. A spokesperson for Microsoft, which owns Minecraft, said: “Our goal is to make Minecraft one of the hardest places for bad actors to operate, and we have a variety of tools we use to help foster a safe online experience, such as parental controls, chat filtering, in-game reporting, and dedicated moderation teams.”

They may also search social media groups that focus on mental health issues such as eating disorders or suicide ideation because they are specifically looking for vulnerable children to target and groom, child safety experts interviewed said.

Perpetrators of sadistic online exploitation who coerce children into inflicting other forms of harm on themselves fall outside those reports.

The coerced abuses can include forcing children to cut themselves, eat their own vomit, drink urine, die by suicide, or harm other children and pets in their homes. Some groups also engage in mass casualty planning, doxing and cyber-harassment.

A common form of abuse is forcing victims to carve perpetrators’ social media usernames on their bodies, said Amanda Goharian, director of research at Thorn, a child safety non-profit.

“One of the key things in these groups is that the level of severity of the harms that are being committed are extreme, and they can rapidly develop into outcomes such as completed suicides,” said Goharian.

In February, the FBI issued a warning to parents and caregivers about the “sharp increase” in the activity of violent online networks, including the “764” group, targeting and exploiting children and other vulnerable individuals.

“The term nihilistic violent extremism (NVE) refers to what is motivating most of these subjects to commit criminal acts. They want to sow chaos and facilitate the destruction of society,” said the letter. “These subjects may be engaging in criminal activity for sexual gratification, social status, a sense of belonging, or for a mix of other reasons.”

The predators target vulnerable children who have disclosed personal information online about the struggles they face, such as mental health challenges, eating disorders, self-harm or suicidal ideation, via gaming or social media platforms. These children are looking for connections and support online, which can render them more susceptible to manipulation, said the experts interviewed.

Predators typically invite victims to move the conversation to a messaging app such as Telegram, which, according to Rifenbark, “doesn’t report very much” to the NCMEC, meaning the organization has limited visibility into potential child sexual exploitation.

It’s on these messaging platforms where the sadistic online exploitation takes place. Telegram did not respond to a request for comment.

Many of the perpetrators have troubled lives or are socially isolated in real life, and are looking for notoriety online as a form of empowerment and attention from others, experts interviewed said.

“These groups are focused on harming children and targeting children for the purposes of clout and like gaining hierarchy and power both within their groups and over the victims,” said Maria Thomas, investigator at Rise Information Security Foundation, a non-profit focused on cybersecurity. “Teens join them because it’s seen as a subversive and edgy thing to do.”

The perpetrators gain status within their groups from posting evidence of their abuse, images such as dossiers of photos and screenshots of messages with their victims, which are known as “lore books”. Typically, these collections are uploaded on to a Telegram channel labeled as “Victim Name Lore Book”, experts said.

“Harm is perpetrated as a way to essentially gain clout within the network, and to be elevated,” said Goharian.

Organized nihilistic violent extremism and sadistic online exploitation were first documented in the 2010s, when a youth cybergang called the Com, which focused on hacking and identity theft before some subsets shifted into child exploitation. Splinter networks, such as CVLT, an online group that espoused neo-Nazism, nihilism and pedophilia as its core principles, were formed. The group 764 was formed in 2021 by a teenager in Texas who had learned grooming and exploitation methods as a member of CVLT.

Online exploitation can escalate into dramatic real-world violence, experts warn. In February, an 18-year-old man from Pennsylvania was arrested and charged with grooming a 13-year-old girl he met on social gaming platform Discord, allegedly convincing her to send him sexually explicit material of herself and images of self-harm. The man travelled across the country to engage in sexual activity with the girl in a motel, according to the US justice department. “Discord has a longstanding policy on Teen and Child Safety that prohibits sexual exploitation and predatory behavior, including content or communities that facilitate grooming,” said a Discord spokesperson.

“When law enforcement arrived at the motel room, they encountered [the man] and the victim and found condoms, a knife, lubricant, razor blades, bloody tissues,” stated a Department of Justice press release about the case. “The victim told law enforcement that [the man] and she had engaged in sexual conduct and that he had used a knife to repeatedly cut her.”

In May, a 16-year-old boy from South Carolina was arrested for engaging in exploitation tied to 764 after receiving a tip from Discord regarding possible school violence.

“Offenders have a very, very high level of control over the children. They are using that connection that they’ve built with that child to really try to control that child to do some pretty harmful things to themselves,” said Rifenbark.

Rifenbark said the NCMEC had received reports from parents who discovered what was happening to their child and took away the child’s phone, only for the child to find another device in the home, such as an old device the family was no longer using, and use it to re-engage with the offenders online, probably due to the high levels of control and grooming they have been subjected to.

“A lot of times when victims are coming and reporting to us, they’re very scared, they’re scared of retaliation from offenders, they’re worried that their personal information might get leaked, because that’s something that the offenders are doing as part of their victimization,” Rifenbark said.

According to Goharian, the networks are highly persistent, with individual perpetrators often operating hundreds of accounts. If one account is reported for violating a platform’s terms of service and removed, they can quickly switch to “shadow” accounts that are already established and immediately resume their activity.

According to a state prosecutor, speaking on the condition of anonymity out of concern for professional reprisals, some juveniles who commit sexual abuse do not outgrow the behavior but often go on to offend as adults. Better interventions are needed for young offenders before their behavior escalates, they said.

“We just do a really bad job sometimes of recognizing dangerous kids,” said the prosecutor. “I don’t think these kids are going to grow out of it. I think they’re growing into it.”

In the US, call or text the Childhelp abuse hotline on 800-422-4453 or visit their website for more resources and to report child abuse or DM for help. For adult survivors of child abuse, help is available at ascasupport.org. In the UK, the NSPCC offers support to children on 0800 1111, and adults concerned about a child on 0808 800 5000. The National Association for People Abused in Childhood (Napac) offers support for adult survivors on 0808 801 0331. In Australia, children, young adults, parents and teachers can contact the Kids Helpline on 1800 55 1800, or Bravehearts on 1800 272 831, and adult survivors can contact Blue Knot Foundation on 1300 657 380. Other sources of help can be found at Child Helplines International

title: Villa sign Wolves midfielder Gomes in £38m deal
description: Aston Villa move for Brazil midfielder Joao Gomes after Amadou Onana suffered a serious knee injury at the World Cup.
fullText:
Aston Villa have completed the £38m signing of midfielder Joao Gomes from Wolves.

The Brazil international joins for an initial £34m with £4m in add-ons.

Villa completed a club record deal worth more than £50m for Freiburg midfielder Johan Manzambi on Friday, beating Newcastle to the Switzerland international.

Atletico Madrid had also been keen on Gomes, but the 25-year-old left Wolves' training camp in Portugal on Thursday to have a medical and finalise his move to Villa Park.

Villa midfielder Amadou Onana is out until next year after suffering a serious knee injury with Belgium at the World Cup.

Villa have also sold Youri Tielemans to Manchester United for £35m, while fellow midfielder Morgan Rogers is set to leave after a £117m deal was agreed with Chelsea.

Gomes made 41 appearances for Wolves last season and was named Player of the Year as they finished bottom of the Premier League.

He played 130 times for the club, scoring seven goals, after joining from Flamengo in 2023.

Wolves technical director Matt Jackson said: "Joao deserves everything he gets from the game. He's a model professional, a lovely young man and a great family man.

"He's the type of professional coaches love and players love, so he will go on to be the outstanding player that we know he is.

"We're obviously sorry to see him go, but we have to take responsible decisions for the football club, and then we build to be stronger collectively.

"It's a very natural part of football - the best run football clubs who don't have unlimited resources have to work this way.

"The model is something that we have to adopt, and our fans have always been hugely understanding of that."

Villa needed to move after Onana suffered an anterior cruciate ligament injury on duty at the World Cup with Belgium, which threw a major spanner in Villa's transfer plans this summer.

With Tielemans being sold, Villa had a gaping hole in their midfield, even if Boubacar Kamara is due to return from his own serious knee injury, which he suffered in January.

Gomes arrives with the experience and Premier League know-how to hit the ground running under manager Unai Emery.

Emery wants ready-made players equipped to understand his methods quickly and Gomes should fit the bill well.

A combative player, he made the third most tackles in the Premier League last season with 107, behind Everton's James Garner and Joao Palhinha, who was on loan at Tottenham from Bayern Munich and whom Villa also hold an interest in this summer.

Gomes' ball recoveries were the fourth highest in the division - he won possession 194 times.

With 116 Premier League appearances in his three and a half years at Molineux, there is plenty of experience as Villa move into a new era following last season's Europa League win.

Gomes arrives at a fair price with Villa needing to walk the financial tightrope to ensure they comply with Premier League and Uefa financial regulations.

Joao Gomes joins Villa with a statistical profile that shows exactly why the club have targeted him. Last season he was one of the Premier League's most active ball winners. He ranked third for tackles and second for both duels contested and duels won.

He also finished eighth for interceptions, which highlights how often he broke up play and how consistently he operated in the heart of Wolves' midfield.

The Brazilian's intensity without the ball was just as impressive. Gomes made more than 1,800 attempts to close down opponents, which placed him among the league's busiest pressers.

Villa's new midfielder was also trusted to take the ball in tight areas for now‑relegated Wolves, ranking sixth in the league for passes received under pressure.

One underrated element is his reliability. The best ability is availability - and he featured in 35 of Wolves' 38 league games.

His accuracy in those moments, almost 84%, is not elite but respectable. It is secure enough in possession for a high intensity midfielder.

On the ball, Gomes tends to carry play forward rather than rely on long or ambitious passing.

He ranked 14th for progressive carries and, although his forward passing numbers were lower, he did lead all Premier League midfielders for line-breaking passes played in the final third.

The only clear flaw in his profile is discipline. He conceded 69 fouls - the most in the league. He is combative, sometimes over zealous, but in bringing him to Villa Park, Unai Emery will believe that edge and ability to win the ball back will strengthen his side's midfield.

title: Best goal, best player, best fans - BBC reporters' World Cup awards
description: BBC Sport reporters pick their best moments of the tournament - and you can vote for yours.
fullText:
After delivering unforgettable drama and surprises across 104 matches over almost six weeks, the World Cup is over.

History has been scripted, records broken and heroes old and new made their mark at football's most coveted tournament.

Read our reporters' reflections from the US, Canada and Mexico and then choose from our chief football writer Phil McNulty's selections for best goal and best save.

Spain captain Rodri won the official World Cup Golden Ball trophy as the tournament's best player.

Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Surprise, surprise. Lionel Messi. Not simply a swansong for a 39-year-old closing down his career, but the enduring inspiration of a nation and a football team. Look at how he controlled the game and pulled the strings when Argentina chased England down in a dramatic semi-final.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

I was in a Kylian Mbappe news conference early in the World Cup and I remember thinking that I'd never seen him looking more relaxed, more confident and more determined to make this his tournament. That demeanour off the pitch was reflected in some brilliant performances on the pitch - great goals, leadership, flair, composure under pressure and more work off the ball than I've ever seen from him.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The player of the tournament has been Jude Bellingham. The Real Madrid midfielder dragged England through by sheer force of will in some games and took his game to another level during the tournament.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Lionel Messi's leadership, football intelligence and ability to decide the biggest matches has proven vital for Argentina. He was the driving force behind their run to the final and that makes him the competition's standout player.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

With eight goals and four assists in eight games at this World Cup, it has to be Lionel Messi. England did their best to keep him quiet in the semi-final and succeeded in the first half, but Messi often finds a way and his two assists proved decisive.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Michael Olise. Marked out of the semi-final against Spain but the former Crystal Palace forward, who started his journey aged six at Hayes & Yeading United, has left his mark on this tournament with his seven assists. At just 24, Olise has another World Cup or two up his sleeve. I'm still playing replays in my mind of that spectacular scissor-kick against Sweden in the last 32. The whole press box erupted thinking it was going in, only for the ball to crash against a post.

Rodri. He has proved to be irreplaceable for his country as he is for Manchester City. Exceptional and so effective at the heart of the best team in the tournament

Easy choice but it has to be Lionel Messi. Doing this at the age of 39 is almost defying the law of physics. He is cementing his place as the greatest.

Spain's 19-year-old centre-back Pau Cubarsi received the Fifa award for the tournament's best young player after playing every minute of their World Cup campaign.

He was a key figure in the Spain defence that conceded only one goal all tournament.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

I am going for Spain's 19-year-old central defender Pau Cubarsi. The Barcelona youngster was magnificent against France in the semi-final and is a star of the future.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Lille fans, regular viewers of Ligue 1 and recruitment teams from the top clubs knew all about his potential but Morocco's Ayyoub Bouaddi burst on to the world stage in style with an exceptional performance in their opening match against Brazil. At the New York New Jersey Stadium that day, I found myself not watching the ball but watching his incredibly intelligent movement around the pitch, which reminded me of a cross between Patrick Vieira and Luka Modric.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

It sounds strange to call Erling Haaland a breakout star but it is hard to explain the impact he has had here in the US. He has become a household name and even more of a global superstar.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cape Verde's 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha. He began his unforgettable World Cup with seven sensational saves in a 0–0 draw against Spain in their debut World Cup match and ended it with eight more remarkable saves as Cape Verde pushed Lionel Messi and Argentina to the brink of elimination. Vozinha's heroics sparked a global following, with his Instagram audience skyrocketing from about 50,000 before the tournament to more than 29 million.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

It seemed like the USA would go far at this tournament, particularly with the wave of support behind them, but it wasn't to be as they exited at the last-16 stage. Despite that, many of their players did their own reputations no harm and one of those was full-back Alex Freeman, with his performances leading to USA boss Mauricio Pochettino saying he could be one of the best in the world in his position.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Step forward Switzerland's Johan Manzambi. Some 20-year-olds might be overwhelmed playing on the biggest stage of all. Not Manzambi. This huge talent plays with a smile on his face and is a natural entertainer who departed the tournament with three goals in four games.

Johan Manzambi. I highlighted him before the World Cup and, although he missed the end for Switzerland through injury, the fact he is moving to the Premier League with Aston Villa for a fee in the region of £50m underlines his quality.

The 17-year-old Mexico midfielder Gilberto Mora, who ended up in their starting line-up. Had the air of Luka Modric and seems destined for the top of the game.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The Azteca for Mexico v England. Might just be the best atmosphere I have ever experienced in seven World Cups. It was an assault on the senses and the singing of the Mexican national anthem was a spine-tingling experience. A simply unforgettable night.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Speaking to the Paraguay fans outside the Boston stadium, moments after one of the most dramatic penalty shootouts I have ever seen, as the South American side stunned Germany. One father and son, draped in Paraguay flags, were in tears as they told me that this was the best day of their lives, and it summed up what special moments at a World Cup can mean to people. I will never forget the emotion of those fans streaming out of the ground in disbelief.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Lionel Messi scoring a hat-trick in his first game at this World Cup showed that he was not done and was going to try to push Argentina to another tournament win.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Sidny Cabral's stunning 103rd-minute equaliser against Argentina. His strike from the edge of the penalty area that flew beyond Emiliano Martinez sent the stadium into disbelief and brought Cape Verde level at 2-2 in extra time. Although Argentina eventually edged the tie 3-2, Cabral's wonder goal became an instant classic and will always be talked about.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

For me it was Argentina being pushed by Cape Verde all the way before the debutants were knocked out. They showed the sort of guts and desire we want to see from these teams that gained the opportunity to play at a World Cup because of the expanded format.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

On my travels around the US, where I have covered 13 games in six different cities, I have spoken to dozens of fans. I was offered a beer and sausages cooked from the back of a pick-up truck before France-Paraguay in Philadelphia, and been stopped for selfies. "You're from the BBC? I have to have a selfie with the guy from the BBC." I have lost count of the number of times I have stopped a fan and introduced myself, only to be asked: "Is it coming home?"

For the moment I actually witnessed, it would have to be Sidny Cabral and his sensational equaliser against Argentina as Cape Verde, the smallest nation to ever reach the knockout stages, stunned the defending champions for a second time to make it 2-2 in Miami.

Cabral's astonishing goal against Argentina. It was as funny as it was breathtaking.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Brazil. Slow. Uninspired. Un-Brazilian. Trudged through the World Cup under Carlo Ancelotti and every weakness was exposed when they deservedly went out to Norway in the last 16.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Senegal came to the tournament with the most talented squad in their history but various problems off the pitch arguably meant the players were not given the right environment to thrive. When they were 2-0 up in the last-32 match against Belgium, it looked as though they would salvage something from this World Cup, but tactical errors and poor game management cost them. It was a massive wasted opportunity for a gifted generation of Senegal players.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Scotland missing out on third-placed qualification was a disappointment and having to wait a couple of days before it was confirmed must have been painful.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cristiano Ronaldo and Portugal. As one of the pre-tournament favourites, they fell short after a 1–0 last-16 defeat by Spain. Ronaldo managed three goals in five matches and, despite high expectations, Portugal never reached the level many expected, ending another World Cup campaign in frustrating fashion.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

The USA. They started this tournament so well and created a real wave of optimism and hope among the home fans, but all that was undone by the controversy surrounding Folarin Balogun and then their limp exit at the hands of Belgium.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

The final being held at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Yes, it is huge but it is not fan-friendly and it is an expensive pain to get to and get away from. Philadelphia would have been my pick. A stadium on the outskirts of the city in a sports complex. The MetLife is a giant bowl in the middle of nowhere.

France in their semi-final defeat by Spain. I tipped Spain to beat France but still did not expect such a flat performance and their star-studded frontline to flounder as badly as they did.

I really thought Brazil under Ancelotti would go further. And clearly so did they.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Mexico. The sheer passion and love for their team and football was incredible. Also such friendly, welcoming fans and people. Incredibly warm and gracious in defeat.

We were in a restaurant in Mexico City the night before they played England, and an MC went around each table asking which country people were from. When we told them we were from England, it was announced to the rest of the diners, who burst into a spontaneous outbreak of applause.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

A word for the Curacao fans I was with inside the Philadelphia Stadium - because who knew that supporters from a nation of just 150,000 could make that much noise? Was there anyone left on the island? But the Brazil fans win it for me because they do not just take over stadiums; they take over cities; Philadelphia felt like Rio or Sao Paulo for 48 hours when they were in town.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The Colombia fans took over Arrowhead Stadium in Kansas City when they played Ghana, and made it feel like a party.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's fans were brilliant, with their legendary Viking row taking over the World Cup. The chant became a global sensation and created an unforgettable atmosphere in every stadium they entered. The most iconic moments came after matches when the players joined the fans to lead the Viking row from the pitch, uniting thousands of supporters in celebration.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

I was based mainly in Los Angeles for the World Cup. There's a large Mexican population there and from what I experienced, they were undoubtedly the best supporters. They were loud - very loud - for every Mexico game and when they won fireworks could be heard around LA while their distinct green jerseys were seen everywhere.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's Viking row was fun to watch, with fans wearing red shirts sitting in lines and rowing in rhythm. The Norway supporters also provided an amusing moment during the quarter-final with England. They sang "You're going home, you're going home, England's going home" as they led 1-0 in Miami. Moments later Jude Bellingham equalised.

The Mexicans. I was fortunate to experience two matches in the Azteca. That is where the heart of this World Cup was and the support for their team was a joy to behold.

Argentina. On two occasions in Miami and Kansas City, I have been in stadiums where their support has been in excess of 60,000 fans. The sheer scale of numbers is matched by their wild passion. They sing constantly and have generated the best atmospheres I have encountered in the US.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The deadlock was broken by Lionel Messi for Argentina against Cape Verde with a triumph of technique as he showed exquisite touch to take a difficult pass on the run before, with little room to work with, he guided it into the top corner with his second touch.

In the same game, Sidny Cabral's extra-time equaliser was a wonderful moment. From the angle just inside the area, his right-foot curling drive into the top corner gave Emiliano Martinez no chance.

In the group stages, Kerim Alajbegovic's goal for Bosnia-Herzegovina against Qatar was a superb solo effort - a weaving run before a rising right-foot shot of high class.

What looked like a casual swing of Erling Haaland's left boot was in fact a perfectly placed finish with astonishing power to crown a match-winning performance for Norway against Brazil in the last 16.

A tight quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland was broken open by a moment of genius from Julian Alvarez. The vastly under-rated attacker provided with a swerving right-footed drive from long range into the top corner in extra time.

Antonio Nusa's opener for Norway against Ivory Coast was perfection. A little shimmy in the area to feint away from a defender, then a precise curling finish into the top corner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Johny Placide made a superb double save for Haiti against Morocco. It came first with his legs then he showed swift reactions to save one-handed to his left.

At the Azteca Stadium, Jordan Pickford had an outstanding game in England's memorable win over Mexico. His early save, diving low to his left to keep out Raul Jimenez's header, was a crucial moment of brilliance.

Orjan Nyland produced a remarkable stop for Norway against Brazil from his own defender Kristoffer Ajer, who inadvertently directed the ball goalwards. Nyland scrambled backwards showing remarkably anticipation and agility to leap and turn the ball against the post.

Aymen Dahmen showed stunning reactions for Tunisia against Japan from Takehiro Tomiyasu's close-range effort, somehow keeping the ball out as it did everything but cross the line.

There was a brilliant diving save by Patrick Beach for Australia in their group game with Turkey, diving away to his right to touch Abdulkerim Bardakci's rising 30-yard shot on to the post.

Iran's Alireza Beiranvand produced a remarkable save from Maxim de Cuyper of Belgium. Beiranvand could not reach Kevin de Bruyne's shot but somehow recovered his position and reacted to keep out a shot from inside the six-yard box.

Mbappe first to win Golden Boot twice as Rodri named best player

Lamine Yamal 'completes football' at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

World Cup is over, but the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Why Beckham was face of World Cup in the US
description: After arriving in Los Angeles 19 years ago promising to grow the game, David Beckham is still helping sell football to America.
fullText:
In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham.

During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss.

On the pitch, the tournament belongs to stars like Kylian Mbappe, Jude Bellingham, Lamine Yamal and Lionel Messi.

Off the pitch, Sir David has become the face of the World Cup, fronting advertising campaigns for 11 brands ranging from McDonald's and Major League Soccer to Stella Artois, Home Depot and Bank of America.

And if you haven't seen him during one of the many half-time commercial breaks, you've probably spotted him at the United States' opening match in Los Angeles, watching alongside his friend Tom Cruise.

Just hours before that, Beckham had become the first professional footballer to receive a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, with actor Cruise once again by his side.

More recently, he was seen singing Wonderwall and Hey Jude as he celebrated Jude Bellingham's two goals against Norway that sent England into the semi-finals.

"David speaks to so many different people. I think that's what's unique about him," said Stacy Jones, founder and CEO of the marketing agency Hollywood Branded.

"He brings in the female demographic because he is so sexy - but he appeals to men. He's an honest, good, authentic, down-to-earth type of individual. So you don't really get affronted by him."

She felt Beckham had achieved something few celebrities manage, moving effortlessly between football, fashion, entertainment, business and the cult of celebrity.

On Hermosa Beach, south of Los Angeles, hundreds of fans gathered to watch Spain reach the World Cup final at an event organised by LA Galaxy - the club Beckham joined in 2007 from Real Madrid.

Ask fans what comes to mind when they hear the name David Beckham and the answers come quickly.

"David Beckham was a really good player for England, but he's doing too many commercials now," said one football fan.

For Megan Hapeman, it's the recent McDonald's advert in which Beckham hopes to receive a cup featuring his own face.

Her six-year-old son Oliver proudly told me he still has one.

"When I got it, I was pretty proud," he said. "Because Mommy liked it, I liked it too."

That's exactly what brands are buying.

Beckham transcends clubs, generations and even football itself.

That broad appeal didn't happen by accident. It was built on Beckham's football legacy in America.

He remains the player with the trademark free-kicks, part of Manchester United's Treble-winning side in 1999, England captain and one of Real Madrid's original Galácticos.

Dan Courtemanche, Major League Soccer's executive vice-president and chief communications officer, admitted Beckham's influence on the game in America cannot be overstated.

"I always tell people he changed the fate of American soccer. Not once, but twice," he said.

When Beckham signed for LA Galaxy 19 years ago, stadiums across Major League Soccer sold out before he had even played a competitive match.

The league needed credibility and he gave it something even broader: mainstream celebrity.

Sixteen years later, Beckham helped persuade Argentina icon Messi to choose Inter Miami over rival offers globally - another moment that transformed MLS, according to Courtemanche.

"Lionel Messi decided to make Major League Soccer his league of choice and Inter Miami his club of choice," he added. "Again, it took us to a whole other level."

Tom Braun, LA Galaxy's president and chief operating officer, said Beckham's influence has stretched way beyond football.

"David's an iconic figure in our sport and our culture," he explained.

"He's built a business around his brand and continues to resonate with people."

The evolution of the 51-year-old, who also played for AC Milan and Paris St-Germain before retiring in 2013, was perhaps best symbolised this summer on Hollywood Boulevard.

When Beckham accepted his star on the Walk of Fame, he reflected he had never imagined "a working-class English footballer" would receive such an honour.

The recognition celebrated not only the life of Beckham the footballer, but Beckham the cultural figure.

"David Beckham, to America, is the World Cup," Jones said.

Having arrived in Los Angeles promising to grow the game, Beckham is still helping sell football to America.

Only now, he's doing it not with a free-kick, but with a brand.

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Could Infantino really push through a 64-team World Cup?
description: If we have learned one thing from Gianni Infantino's Fifa tenure, it is that more is better. That could mean 64 teams at the World Cup.
fullText:
If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better.

More tournaments, more games, more money.

The new-look Club World Cup, the expanded World Cup, the inflated ticket prices.

And, of course, the Super Bowl-style show that meant the half-time break at the World Cup final lasted 27 minutes and 22 seconds.

Infantino appears to have thrown the door wide open to a 64-team World Cup.

"If you don't give smaller countries a chance to participate, they'll lack the incentive to keep improving," he said.

Except, of course, smaller countries are not denied the chance. If they are good enough, they qualify. See Cape Verde, Curacao, Jordan and Uzbekistan.

Purely in terms of the competition format, it does make sense.

The top two teams in the 16 groups would go through to the knockout stage. No longer would there be a need to send a few third-placed teams through.

But is there really any other logical reason to expand to 64 teams?

Let's start with how we got here.

The 2030 World Cup will mark 100 years since the first World Cup, which was held in Uruguay.

The tournament will primarily be hosted by Morocco, Portugal and Spain - but the opening three matches will be played in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay as centenary hosts.

Conmebol - the South American confederation - wants more games.

In April 2025, it requested a one-off tournament of 64 teams so it could take on the full programme of the additional four groups.

Its president, Alejandro Dominguez, said it would ensure "nobody on the planet is left out of the party".

There has been little support from elsewhere.

Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin dismissed it as a "bad idea" for both the tournament and qualifying process.

Victor Montagliani - president of the governing body of North and Central America and the Caribbean (Concacaf) - said last year it would damage "the broader football ecosystem".

Asian Football Confederation (AFC) president Sheikh Salman bin Ibrahim Al Khalifa agreed, saying further expansion could bring "chaos".

None of which means it will not happen, with the decision taken by the 37 members of the Fifa Council.

A 64-team tournament has two main plus points: a more natural format, and more opportunities for emerging nations.

It is the latter which Infantino is championing, especially after the heroics of Cape Verde.

Fifa faced criticism before the tournament, with suggestions the additional teams would not be competitive.

Then Cape Verde drew with Spain and Uruguay to finish second in their group. And the Blue Sharks gave Argentina an almighty scare by taking them to extra time in the last 16, before losing 3-2.

But the fairytale stories must be balanced with the competitive edge of the tournament.

Haiti, Iraq, Jordan, Panama, Tunisia and Uzbekistan failed to pick up a point - and this summer's group stage had minimal jeopardy for the top nations.

Add more teams and it would be difficult to see a repeat of top-four seeds like Belgium (2022), Germany (2018), Spain (2014), Italy (2010) and France (2002) failing to make to knockout stage.

Then there is the question of where the extra places would go.

Based on this year's qualifying, these are the additional countries who would have made it into a 64-team World Cup 2026 (with their Fifa ranking at the start of the tournament in brackets):

AFC: Indonesia (118), Oman (79), United Arab Emirates (68).

Uefa: Denmark (21), Italy (12), Poland (36).

Concacaf: Honduras (65), Jamaica (71), Suriname (125).

Conmebol: Bolivia (77), Venezuela (49).

Africa: Burkina Faso (62), Cameroon (44), Gabon (86), Nigeria (26).

Six of those teams are from the top 50 of Fifa's world rankings - three from Europe, two from Africa and one from South America.

And therein lies the problem. Asia and Concacaf are weaker confederations, as was proven by performances this year.

Nine of Africa's 10 countries got out of the group stage. But only two of nine Asian countries advanced, and just three of the six Concacaf countries - the co-hosts - made it through.

New Zealand, who got one point and finished bottom of their group, are the only country from Oceania in the world's top 150.

There is an argument that if you extend again, you should give a greater weight of places to Europe just for competitive balance.

But, on the other hand, there are those who would argue that bringing more teams into the World Cup ultimately allows the game to develop further in those countries.

Infantino will sell the expansion as being good for the global game, but there have to be doubts that it is good for the competition.

Almost a third of Fifa's 211 nations would be present.

Would the group stage not simply become a glorified pan-confederation qualifying competition for what is, essentially, a knockout tournament?

Expanding the World Cup to 64 teams is really just a case of bolting on four additional four-team groups.

But more groups means more time.

This year's tournament lasted 39 days. An additional 24 matches would require 20 schedule slots. Or another five days.

But this summer's event was unique for two reasons: multiple time zones and indoor stadiums.

Fifa was able to elongate a matchday at either end, with games scheduled over a 14-hour period so it could stay true to exclusive time windows for television coverage.

That will not be so straightforward in the future, even with 48 teams.

Climate change is making playing in the daytime more challenging - see the record heatwaves in Europe in recent weeks.

Other countries are far less likely to have the covered venues of Atlanta, Dallas Houston and Vancouver to mitigate it.

The Saudi Arabia World Cup is likely to move to January 2035 but it is not clear how other countries will get around this growing problem.

Until this year, only once had there been dual hosts of the World Cup - Japan and South Korea in 2002.

It is hard to imagine any one country ever being capable of hosting again. Even Saudi Arabia.

Could we be looking at World Cups hosted by, for instance, the UK and Scandinavia? Or Eastern Europe?

How would an Asian or African country be able to fund hosting the World Cup?

Let's look a just a few issues that would need to be resolved.

This summer there were 16 host cities with stadiums having capacities ranging from 43,000 to 81,000. More would be required.

There would need to be 64 individual, elite-level training facilities for the qualifying nations.

Then there is the provision of hotels for the fans, and the infrastructure and support network to move them around.

Expanding the World Cup is not as simple as giving more opportunities to more countries.

One of Infantino's key promises when he was elected was to bring more money into the game.

Every football association now has access to $8m (£5.9m) in funding over a four-year period, with a further $1.2m (£890,000) on top for the smaller nations.

Confederations get $60m (£44.5m) to develop, promote and organise football.

It is why Infantino is likely to be re-elected unopposed next year.

This money has to come from somewhere, primarily the World Cup.

In 2017, Fifa projected a 48-team World Cup would bring in $6.5bn (£4.8bn).

In 2022, it revised the format again and added 24 games. Now, it expects to rake in $9bn (£6.7bn) this year.

A 64-team event is likely to raise more from television, sponsorship and ticket sales.

Fifa says it has a responsibility to grow the game, and its tool for this appears to be more and more football.

But critics would argue there has to be a point where expansion is no longer good for the game.

Why European backlash over Trump intervention won't worry Infantino

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Factbox-Authorities restricting data centres amid AI boom
description: July 20 (Reuters) - A growing number of governments, regulators and cities around the world are moving to freeze, restrict or ban new data centre construction, as concerns mount over electricity costs
fullText:
July 20 (Reuters) - A growing number of governments, regulators and cities around the world are moving to freeze, restrict or ban new data centre construction, as concerns mount over electricity costs, strained ‌water supplies, land scarcity and the burden on local communities from the infrastructure powering the AI boom.

Governor Kathy Hochul imposed a one-year construction moratorium on data centres using 50 megawatts or more of ​power, making New York the first U.S. state to enact a full moratorium.

During the freeze, the state's Department of Environmental Conservation will not issue new discretionary permits while officials develop standards to assess the environmental impact of data centres.

Governor Janet Mills vetoed bipartisan legislation that would have enacted an 18-month moratorium on new data centres using more than 20 ‌MW of power.

Mills said she supported a ⁠moratorium in principle but objected to the bill's failure to carve out an exception for a specific project in the Town of Jay.

MONTEREY PARK, CALIFORNIA, U.S.

Residents voted to permanently ban ⁠data centres in Monterey Park in June 2026, becoming the first U.S. city to do so at the ballot box, following public backlash over a planned facility.

The Dutch capital imposed a one-year moratorium on new data centre developments in 2019. ​In April ​2025, it barred new data centres or expansions in the ​municipality until at least 2030.

Separately, the Dutch government's ‌2022 hyperscale ban restricts large facilities to two designated locations nationally, though Microsoft won approval in January 2026 for a project split into three towers, each individually below the size threshold.

DUBLIN, IRELAND (restriction now lifted)

Ireland's grid operator effectively blocked new data centre connections around Dublin from 2021 after warnings that the facilities were straining the grid.

The freeze ended in December 2025, with new connections now required to bring their own on-site power generation.

AUSTRALIA (planned legislation)

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese ‌said Australia would establish a government office to manage the development ​of AI standards, including rules for large data centres covering their location, ​power and water use, with legislation due early ​next year.

Australia does not currently have specific AI laws, relying instead on a range of ‌privacy and consumer protection laws and a voluntary ​AI ethics framework.

Denmark's government has ​introduced a draft bill to its parliament that would give new data centres the lowest priority for power grid connections amid growing capacity shortages.

The proposed law, backed by a broad cross-party agreement representing 80% of ​the parliament, would prioritise households, healthcare, ‌industry, transport and renewable energy projects instead.

Denmark's power grid has come under strain from a surge ​in electricity demand and connection requests, including from large data centres.

title: Fernandez sent off as Argentina lose World Cup final
description: Enzo Fernandez's 2026 World Cup ended in misery as the Chelsea midfielder was sent off in Argentina's final defeat to Spain.  The 25-year-old had already been booked for showing dissent towards referee Slavko Vincic when he made a poor challenge on Pau Cubarsi in the last minute of normal time.  Fernandez was subsequently sent off and received Argentina's first red card at a World Cup since 2006.
fullText:
Enzo Fernandez's 2026 World Cup ended in misery as the Chelsea midfielder was sent off in Argentina's final defeat to Spain.

The 25-year-old had already been booked for showing dissent towards referee Slavko Vincic when he made a poor challenge on Pau Cubarsi in the last minute of normal time.

Fernandez was subsequently sent off and received Argentina's first red card at a World Cup since 2006.

BBC co-commentator Alan Shearer said: "How daft is that from Enzo Fernandez?

"He is on a yellow card for dissent, not the tackle, but for dissent. He knows that, because that didn't happen long ago. For him to go into a challenge like that is crazy.

"He can protest as much as he wants, but it's the right decision. He gave the referee only one choice, and that was to show another yellow there for the red."

Fernandez had played a key role in helping his nation reach the final where they had the chance to defend their world title.

He will now have three weeks to rest and recover, as per Fifa guidelines, before returning to Stamford Bridge for the new 2026-27 Premier League season under new manager Xabi Alonso.

Chelsea kick off the new season at Fulham on Monday, 24 August.

title: Money, Geopolitics And Power Dominated The World Cup; Same Again In 2030?
description: The World Cup was widely considered to be the most controversial in history, but it simply continued a recent trend that looks set to continue in 2030 when the tournament heads to six countries
fullText:
When FIFA president Gianni Infantino handed over the governing body’s inaugural “Peace Prize” to Donald Trump last December, it seemed to epitomise collective concerns about what this summer’s tournament would become.

In some respects, we were wrong; after all, once the kick-off whistle blew and the soccer began, pre-match diatribe dissipated as people across the world focused on the winners, the scorers and the stars.

Pre-tournament unease about ICE agents attending games, expensive tickets and empty seats faded into the background, and even the U.S. president himself disappeared from view; he didn’t attend any matches, nor did he make any public statements.

That was until Trump’s intervention in the Balogun red card case sounded a wake-up alarm to remind us that this was the 2026 edition of FIFA’s showcase men’s competition, which was always going to be, arguably, the most controversial in history.

But many of us said that about Qatar 2022, where human rights, unsold tickets and a belief among some that the small Gulf nation was an undeserving host dominated the tournament discourse and narrative.

Given the turbulence, complexity and sensitivity of our world, perhaps the tournament is now established as a proxy for expressing global anxieties.

This poses some interesting questions, as does the six-nation hosting of the next men’s World Cup, which may be contested by sixty-four teams, rather than forty-eight at this summer’s edition (or the thirty-two that appeared in Qatar and previous World Cups).

From the U.S./Israel conflict with Iran, which led to immigration restrictions being placed on the latter’s national team, to a Somalian FIFA referee being denied entry to the U.S., it may seem difficult to imagine the 2030 tournament being anywhere near as controversial.

Yet the 2030 edition will take place across six countries: Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay; and Morocco, Portugal and Spain, which will pose logistical challenges and create some troubling juxtapositions.

The 2026 final between Spain and Argentina was characterised as a “Palestine versus Israel” match; the current politics of Spain’s left-wing government standing in stark contrast to the right-wing politics of its counterpart in Buenos Aires.

And the early signs of a smooth 2030 tournament hosting are not promising; the South Americans have been accused of both brutal tactics on the field and racist abuse by fans off it.

Over in the West, trouble is already brewing; Morocco has been making bullish claims about hosting the 2030 final, even going so far as to build the world’s biggest football stadium, even though government officials in Madrid believe their city is the rightful destination for it.

Morocco has trouble along its borders, specifically with Algeria, with which it has a fractious relationship, though there are also ongoing disputes over Western Sahara and territorial claims to it.

In theory, Donald Trump will no longer be in office by 2030, though his influence may endure, not least in Morocco and Spain.

He has a close relationship with the King of Morocco; indeed, the U.S. administration has recently recognized Western Sahara as Moroccan territory, whilst the government in Rabat has cultivated increasingly strong relations with Israel.

Meantime, Trump has recently called Spain a “wasted cause” and a “terrible partner”, both for its perceived role in NATO and its vocal support for Palestine, issues that may ultimately undermine Madrid’s bid to stage the 2030 final, especially if Trump’s and Infantino’s bromance endures.

Adding a further dimension to Trump’s potential influence on the next tournament, his relationship with the right-wing conservative leaders of Argentina and Paraguay - Javier Milei and Santiago Peña respectively – means that we could see similar tactics being used to influence FIFA, sanction rivals, and sanitise the image and reputation of these countries.

Founded in 1904, FIFA’s original mission was to unify national football associations, standardize the laws of the game, and create a centralized international football championship.

When the first World Cup was staged in 1930, its purpose was to establish an independent, global football championship that allowed professional players to compete for a world title, bypassing the Olympic Games’ amateur-only restrictions.

However, since then, the World Cup appears to have morphed into something completely different, as this summer’s tournament became a convergence of big money, geopolitics and power.

The World Cup has been political in the past, for instance, in 1962, when Brazilian player Garrincha was sent off in the semi-final, prompting Chilean President Jorge Alessandri and Peruvian President Manuel Prado to strongly lobby FIFA on the player’s behalf.

Similarly, big money hasn’t been anathema to either the governing body or the tournament, with FIFA’s president, Joao Havelange, partnering in 1974 with Horst Dassler, the chief executive of Adidas, to transform the organisation commercially.

However, the 2026 edition has taken World Cup football into territory it hasn’t previously encountered, as the tournament became the embodiment of neoliberal ideology (dynamic ticket pricing illustrating this) and a geopolitical instrument (Trump’s Balogun intervention being a case in point).

The World Cup is no longer simply a football tournament; it has become a commercial behemoth, a Disneyfied entertainment product, and a nexus for countries and politicians seeking to accumulate and project power.

2030’s tournament will be no different.

In the past, when FIFA’s circus leaves town, most people seem to forget what all the fuss was about; after all, how many people spent this summer discussing migrant worker rights in Qatar (as many had done in 2022)?

But this summer’s tournament feels different; such was the profound nature of the issues often raised that, in 2030, one senses they will recur and intensify as our world becomes a more turbulent, complex and sensitive place.

This article was originally published on Forbes.com
